html:
COMMISSION IMPLEMENTING REGULATION (EU) 2026/1412

establishing revised benchmark values for the free allocation of allowances for the period 2026–2030, pursuant to Article 10a(2) of Directive 2003/87/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council.

THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION HAS ADOPTED THIS REGULATION,

having regard to the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union,

having regard to Directive 2003/87/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 October 2003 establishing a system for emission trading within the Community and amending Council Directive 96/61/EC ( 1 ), in particular Article 10a(2), and

WHEREAS, Decision 2011/278/EU of the Commission ( 2 ) established 54 benchmarks which serve as a basis for free allocation (the "benchmarks"), and the values of these benchmarks for the period 2013–2020; Whereas Delegated Regulation (EU) 2019/331 of the Commission ( 3 ) replaced Decision 2011/278/EU and set out identical starting points for determining annual reduction rates for each update of the benchmark values for the period 2021–2030.

Implementing Regulation (EU) 2021/447 of the Commission ( 4 ) established the revised benchmark values for the period 2021–2025, in accordance with Article 10a(2) of Directive 2003/87/EC. The revised benchmark values for the period 2026–2030 should be based on a new reference period regarding the submission of data and the application of the annual reduction rate. These revised values should therefore be established in this Regulation.

Directive 2003/87/EC was amended by the European Parliament and Council Directive (EU) 2023/959 (5) to align the EU's emissions trading system with the European Parliament and Council Regulation (EU) 2021/1119 (6), which sets a target for a net reduction of emissions of at least 55% by 2030 compared to 1990 levels. Consequently, Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2018/2066 (7) concerning the monitoring and reporting of greenhouse gas emissions was amended by Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2023/2122 (8), and Delegated Regulation (EU) 2019/331 establishing Union-wide transitional rules for harmonized free allocation of allowances was amended by Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2024/873 (9). These amendments to Directive 2003/87/EC, Implementing Regulation (EU) 2018/2066, and Delegated Regulation (EU) 2019/331 are therefore relevant for the establishment of revised benchmark values for the period 2026–2030.

In accordance with Article 10a.2, paragraph (c), of Directive 2003/87/EC, the revised benchmark values for the period 2026–2030 shall be established based on the data on the greenhouse gas efficiency of installations submitted pursuant to Article 11 of that Directive for the years 2021 and 2022. For each benchmark, the average greenhouse gas efficiency for the installations within that benchmark shall be calculated for the years 2021 and 2022. Based on a comparison between the average value for the 10% most efficient installations within each benchmark and the benchmark values specified in Annex I to Delegated Regulation (EU) 2019/331, annual reduction rates shall be established for each benchmark for the 14-year period 2007/2008–2021/2022. These annual reduction rates are then used to calculate the corresponding reductions in the benchmark values for the 20-year period 2007/2008–2027/2028 through extrapolation. In accordance with Article 10a.2, paragraph (d), of Directive 2003/87/EC, the reduction applied during the 20-year period shall not be less than 6% and shall not exceed 50%. Article 10a.2, paragraph (4), lays down specific provisions for updating the benchmark values for aromatics and synthesis gas, whereby the adjustment percentage is aligned with that applicable to the benchmarks for refineries. Since the updates of the benchmark values for refineries and hydrogen were decoupled by Directive (EU) 2023/959, these specific provisions regarding the alignment of the update of the benchmark values for aromatics and synthesis gas with the update of the benchmark values for refineries do not apply to the update of the benchmark values for hydrogen.

As a result of the revised definition of emissions introduced by Directive (EU) 2023/959, and in accordance with Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2026/389 (10), the method for updating benchmark values for sodium carbonate (soda) should take into account the corresponding intermediate emissions that are monitored and reported according to Implementing Regulation (EU) 2018/2066. Therefore, for the period 2026–2030, these emissions should be added to the benchmark value for sodium carbonate (soda) as a final step in the calculation, after applying the annual reduction rate during the 20-year extrapolation period.

To ensure comparability with previous updates of the benchmarks, for the benchmarks listed in section 2 of Annex I to Delegated Regulation (EU) 2019/331 and which were previously affected by the rules on interchangeability between fuel and electricity, the average greenhouse gas efficiency of the 10% most efficient installations within each of these benchmarks should take into account the indirect emissions from electricity consumption of these installations when the greenhouse gas efficiency is used to determine the revised benchmark values for free allocation of emission allowances for the period 2026–2030.

Delegated Regulation (EU) 2024/873 also stipulates that measurable and non-measurable heat produced from electricity should be included in the heat and fuel benchmarks, and this should be taken into account when determining the revised benchmark values applicable for the period 2026–2030.

Furthermore, Delegated Regulation (EU) 2024/873 introduced changes to a number of benchmarks regarding definitions and system boundaries. This regulation opened up the benchmark for sintered iron ore, as well as the benchmarks for white and grey cement clinker, to alternative products made from agglomerated iron ore and alternative hydraulic binders, respectively. Additionally, hydrogen produced through water electrolysis was included in either the hydrogen benchmark or the ammonia benchmark. These changes should be considered when establishing the revised benchmark values for the period 2026-2030.

Delegated Regulation (EU) 2024/873 also modified the benchmark for crude iron by adding steel production using direct reduction technology to the definitions of products covered and the system boundaries within that benchmark. In accordance with Article 10a(2)(e) of Directive 2003/87/EC, the production of steel within the crude iron benchmark using direct reduction technology should not be taken into account when calculating the average greenhouse gas efficiency of installations for the revised benchmark values for the period 2026-2030.

The list of installations containing information relevant to the free allocation of emission allowances was submitted to the Commission by the Member States no later than September 30, 2024, in accordance with Article 11(1) of Directive 2003/87/EC. To ensure that the benchmark values are based on...
tldr: The EU has established new regulations governing how industries can receive free emission allowances between 2026 and 2030. The aim is to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from factories that produce goods such as cement, iron ore, and hydrogen.
lattlast:
The EU has established new regulations governing how companies are allocated emission allowances. For the period from 2026 to 2030, these allowances will be distributed based on the environmental efficiency of factories. This means that factories that successfully reduce their emissions will receive a more favorable position within the system.

These rules affect various industries, including those producing cement, iron ore, and hydrogen. To determine the number of emission allowances each factory receives, data from 2021 and 2022 is analyzed to assess how much they have managed to reduce their emissions over time.

Specific regulations also apply to certain substances, such as sodium carbonate (often called soda), and various types of fuels. The purpose of all these calculations is to ensure that industries in Europe comply with the EU's goals of significantly reducing overall greenhouse gas emissions by 2030.
